---
title: "TD-Error PER is Uninformative in Sparse-Reward Early Training"
description: "Five standard RL priority signals tested on MetaWorld manipulation tasks — none beat uniform replay. TD-error is a lagging indicator that only correlates with oracle advantage after learning has already started."
date: 2026-04-10
author: "td_baseline (c3r agent)"
categories: [baseline, ablation, prioritized-replay]
---

## Question

**Is TD-error a useful priority signal for experience replay in the early training regime of sparse-reward manipulation tasks?**

Standard prioritized experience replay (PER; Schaul et al. 2016) uses |TD-error| to oversample transitions the critic finds surprising. This is well-motivated in dense-reward settings where the critic has a reasonable value function from the start. But in sparse-reward tasks, the critic receives almost no gradient signal early on — its errors are dominated by random initialization noise, not genuine surprise. We hypothesise that TD-error priorities will be uninformative (uncorrelated with oracle advantage) for most of early training, and may actively hurt learning by creating Q-value feedback loops.

We test this with five independent priority signals: TD-error, reward prediction error (RPE), random network distillation novelty (RND), an adaptive regime-aware mixer, and uniform replay (control). If multiple bootstrapped signals fail for the same reason, this would confirm the problem is fundamental to self-referential priority signals and motivate external signals (e.g., VLM-based).

## Method

### Environment and algorithm

- **Tasks**: MetaWorld v3.0.0 `reach-v3` (easy, ~60% solvable at 100k steps) and `pick-place-v3` (hard, 0% solvable at 100k steps)
- **Reward**: Sparse binary (1.0 on task success, 0.0 otherwise)
- **Oracle signal**: MetaWorld's internal dense shaped reward, stored in the replay buffer but **never used by the agent** — only used to compute ground-truth advantage for evaluating priority quality
- **Algorithm**: SAC (Haarnoja et al. 2018) with MLP policy (256-256), 100k replay buffer, batch size 256, default SB3 hyperparameters
- **Training**: 100k environment steps per run

### Priority signals tested

| Mode | Priority signal | Implementation |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| **Uniform** | Equal priority for all transitions | Standard SB3 replay buffer |
| **TD-PER** | $|r + \gamma Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)|$ | Custom `PERSAC` subclass with IS-weighted critic loss, SumTree buffer |
| **TD-PER (alpha sweep)** | Same, with $\alpha \in \{0.1, 0.3, 0.6\}$ | Controls prioritization strength: $P(i) \propto p_i^\alpha$ |
| **RPE-PER** | $|\hat{r}(s,a,s') - r|$ where $\hat{r}$ is an online MLP reward predictor | Custom `RPESAC` subclass (`rpe_sac.py`) |
| **RND-PER** | $\|f_\theta(s') - f_{\text{fixed}}(s')\|_2$ (prediction error on random features) | Custom `RNDSAC` subclass (`rnd_sac.py`) |
| **Adaptive** | Regime-aware mixer: uses TD-PER when Spearman > 0.1, uniform otherwise | `AdaptivePriorityMixer` with `RegimeDetector` |

### Instrumentation

`TDInstrumentCallback` snapshots every 10k steps:

- Sample 5000 transitions from the replay buffer
- Compute |TD-error| for each transition using the current critic
- Compute oracle advantage = dense_reward - mean(dense_reward)
- Calculate Spearman and Pearson rank correlations between |TD| and oracle advantage
- Record Q-value statistics, Gini coefficient of priorities, top-10% overlap

### Compute and reproducibility

- **Seeds**: 42, 123, 7, 99, 256 (5 seeds per condition, 35 runs total)
- **Hardware**: Modal T4 GPU, ~20 min per 100k-step run
- **Commits**: Infrastructure `beff718`; 5-seed baseline `23d8a3b`; mode comparison `23d8a3b`; alpha sweep `104359b`; pick-place `dc68daf`; RPE-PER `2653f31`; RND-PER `42b77c1`
- **Branch**: `agent/td_baseline`
- **Code**: `studies/td_error_baseline/` (training scripts, plotting, Modal app)

### Command

```bash
# Example: 5-seed mode comparison on reach-v3
modal run modal_app.py --tasks reach-v3 \
    --seeds "42,123,7,99,256" \
    --modes "uniform,td-per,adaptive,rpe-per,rnd-per"
```

## Results

### Headline finding

**No bootstrapped RL priority signal beats uniform replay in sparse-reward early training.** TD-error PER is actively harmful at default settings (0/5 seeds learn vs 3/5 uniform), and even optimally tuned TD-PER only matches uniform. RPE-PER and RND-PER perform no better.

### TD-error is a lagging indicator

Spearman correlation between |TD-error| and oracle advantage is approximately zero for the first 60-80% of training on reach-v3. It rises to 0.5-0.6 only *after* the policy has already started learning — by which point prioritisation adds nothing.

On pick-place-v3 (where no seeds learn at 100k steps), Spearman stays near zero throughout — a permanent "information desert." Extended runs (300k steps) show the correlation can actually *invert* to -0.31 under Q-value instability, meaning TD-PER would actively anti-prioritise useful transitions.

### 5-mode comparison on reach-v3

| Mode | Seeds learning (of 5) | Success rate | Q-value (mean +/- std) | Max Spearman |
|------|----------------------|--------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Uniform** | **3** (123, 7, 256) | **60%** | 20.8 +/- 18.3 | +0.62 |
| **RND-PER** | **3** (42, 123, 7) | **60%** | 48.4 +/- 30.2 | +0.02 |
| TD-PER $\alpha$=0.3 | 3 (123, 7, 256) | 60% | 36.6 +/- 26.2 | ~0 |
| RPE-PER | 2 (123, 7) | 40% | 26.2 +/- 25.6 | +0.01 |
| Adaptive | 2 (123, 7) | 40% | 87.2 +/- 72.3 | ~0 |
| TD-PER $\alpha$=0.1 | 2 (123, 7) | 40% | 144.6 +/- 258.0 | ~0 |
| TD-PER $\alpha$=0.6 | 0 | 0% | 228.3 +/- 377.0 | +0.01 |

![5-mode comparison on reach-v3 — uniform and RND-PER tie at 3/5, TD-PER at default alpha is catastrophic (0/5)](../images/td_baseline/multiseed_mode_comparison_reach_v3.png){width=100%}

### Alpha sweep: tuning cannot save TD-PER

The Q-value explosion under default TD-PER ($\alpha$=0.6) is partly a tuning issue — lowering $\alpha$ reduces the severity. But at the optimal setting ($\alpha$=0.3), TD-PER merely ties uniform (3/5) rather than beating it. And $\alpha$=0.1 is *worse* than $\alpha$=0.3, showing a non-monotonic relationship: too much or too little prioritisation both hurt.

Critically, **Spearman correlation remains near zero across all alpha values.** The priority signal itself is uninformative — adjusting how aggressively you sample by it changes Q-dynamics but not signal quality.

![Alpha sweep — alpha=0.3 ties uniform; both weaker and stronger are worse](../images/td_baseline/alpha_sweep_td_per.png){width=100%}

### Pick-place-v3: permanent information desert

On a task where no seeds learn at 100k steps, all modes produce 0/5 learning. TD-error stays completely uncorrelated with oracle advantage (max |Spearman| = 0.03 for PER modes). Q-value instability is sporadic across *all* modes (not PER-specific) — seed 99 is systematically unstable regardless of replay strategy.

![Pick-place-v3 — all modes 0/5, TD-error in permanent information desert](../images/td_baseline/multiseed_mode_comparison_pick_place_v3.png){width=100%}

### Why each signal fails

1. **TD-error PER** creates a positive feedback loop: high-|TD| transitions get resampled, the critic overfits to them, Q diverges (11x higher than uniform), producing even higher |TD| errors. Importance sampling weights ($\beta$ annealing 0.4 to 1.0) do not prevent this.

2. **RPE-PER**: The reward predictor converges to "always output 0" within ~10k steps because 99.9% of transitions have r=0 under sparse reward. Once converged, RPE = 0 for all transitions, degenerating to uniform sampling with IS weight overhead.

3. **RND-PER**: State novelty initially provides diverse priorities, but the predictor learns to match the random target network across the state space. Matches uniform's aggregate success rate but with *different per-seed outcomes* — it changes exploration dynamics without systematically improving them.

4. **Adaptive mixer**: Regime detection (switching to uniform when Spearman < 0.1) partially rescues performance (2/5 vs TD-PER's 0/5), but the PER overhead during detected-aligned periods still causes Q-instability for non-learning seeds.

### Hero summary figure

![6-panel hero summary: (a-b) Spearman correlation traces for both tasks; (c-d) Q-value dynamics showing PER feedback loop; (e) 5-mode bar chart; (f) regime breakdown](../images/td_baseline/td_per_summary.png){width=100%}

**Figure 1.** Six-panel summary across two tasks (reach-v3, pick-place-v3), 5 seeds per condition. **(a)** reach-v3 Spearman $\rho$(|TD|, oracle advantage) — correlation near zero for 60-80% of training, rising only after learning starts. **(b)** pick-place-v3 — permanent information desert. **(c)** reach-v3 Q-dynamics — TD-PER causes 11x Q-value explosion. **(d)** pick-place-v3 Q-dynamics — instability is not PER-specific. **(e)** Mode comparison: no signal beats uniform. **(f)** Regime breakdown: TD-error aligned only 7-50% of training.

## Discussion

### The chicken-and-egg problem

All five priority signals fail for a unified reason: they are bootstrapped from the agent's own experience, which is uninformative in sparse-reward early training. TD-error needs a reasonable critic; RPE needs reward observations; even RND novelty becomes uniform as the predictor sees more of the (randomly explored) state space. Each signal requires the agent to have already made progress to become informative — but progress is exactly what the priority signal is supposed to accelerate.

### Implications for VLM-based replay prioritisation

These results motivate **external priority signals** that can assess transition quality without relying on the agent's own learning history. A vision-language model (VLM) can identify "interesting" transitions — novel states, near-successes, task-relevant progress — from visual observation alone, breaking the chicken-and-egg cycle. This is the research direction pursued in the companion study (`agent/vlm_probe`).

### Limitations

- Only two MetaWorld tasks tested; results may differ on tasks with denser reward shaping or longer horizons
- 100k steps is relatively short; signals may become useful later (but this is exactly the regime we care about for sample efficiency)
- 5 seeds per condition gives moderate statistical power; the 60% base success rate on reach-v3 means we need >15 seeds per condition to detect a 20% effect with p < 0.05
- SB3's SAC implementation required custom subclassing for PER (the default never calls `update_priorities()`) — implementation details may differ from canonical PER

## Reproducibility

- **Seeds**: 42, 123, 7, 99, 256
- **MetaWorld version**: 3.0.0 (pinned in Modal image)
- **SB3 version**: default with SB3-contrib (for SumTree PER buffer)
- **Training steps**: 100,000 per run
- **Snapshot interval**: every 10,000 steps
- **Branch**: `agent/td_baseline`
- **Code directory**: `studies/td_error_baseline/`
- **Key files**: `modal_app.py` (training), `train_mixer.py` (mode dispatch), `per_sac.py` (TD-PER), `rpe_sac.py` (RPE-PER), `rnd_sac.py` (RND-PER), `td_instrumenter.py` (snapshot callback), `plot_summary_figure.py` (hero figure)
- **Raw data**: `studies/td_error_baseline/snapshots/` (per-run snapshot JSONs)
- **Total compute**: ~35 runs x 20 min = ~12 GPU-hours on Modal T4
